main_finding: Around 40 C tumor oxygenation improved, while higher temperatures reduced oxygenation because of vascular restriction
Mild hyperthermia improves oxygenation during and up to 1-2 days after heating and can enhance radiation response
Hyperthermia perturbs multiple DNA repair pathways including HR and NHEJ-related components
Mild hyperthermia inhibits homologous recombination and induces BRCA2 degradation
HR inhibition and radiosensitization depend on thermal dose; 41-43 C for about 30-60 min is a key sensitizing range
Shorter interval caused more residual DNA damage, more apoptosis, and more cell kill; sequence had little effect

model: cell lines organoids xenografts plus patient cohort
main_finding: Radiosensitization critically depended on the HT-RT interval and shorter intervals aligned with better survival
Interval alone was not the dominant predictor once thermal dose and treatment quality were considered
Adequate thermal dose improved complete response; superficial lesions responded better when sufficient heating was achieved
The strongest cytotoxic pattern was 40 C + 4 Gy + 48 h with high apoptosis and reduced VEGF/PDGF
Temperature, duration, thermal dose, interval, and sequence all matter, but thermal dose is among the most reproducible predictors
HTRT improved complete response and loco-regional control over RT without clear excess of severe toxicity

cancer_context: recurrent head and neck cancer after prior RT
main_finding: Repeated HT within 2 h after RT produced high response with manageable toxicity

model: tumor microenvironment and heat response literature
main_finding: The cytotoxic threshold for hyperthermia was described around 42.5 C and the critical temperature for cell-killing curves was between 42.5 C and 43 C
